html:
Hover over sphere for vectors. Orbit to view.
⚠ Figure failed to load

plan.contextChunk:
direction, $\mathbf{q}$, from the surface, the Phong specular
contribution, $\ell_{\mbox{Phong spec}}$, is:
$$\ell_{\mbox{Phong spec}} = k_s (\mathbf{r} \cdot \mathbf{q})^\alpha \ell_{\texttt{in}},$$
where $k_s$ is a constant, $\alpha$ is a parameter governing the spread
of the specular reflection, and the unit vector $\mathbf{r}$ denotes the
direction of maximum specular reflection, given by
$$\mathbf{r} = 2(\mathbf{p} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \mathbf{n} - \mathbf{p}$$

@fig-rendering shows the ambient, Lambertian, and Phong shading components of a sphere
under two-source illumination, and a comparison with a real sphere under
similar real illumination.

:::{layout-ncol="3" #fig-rendering}
![Lambertian](figures/imaging/sphereDiffuse.png){width="90%" #fig-rendering-a}

![Phong](figures/imaging/spherePhongRoughness0.3.png){width="90%" #fig-rendering-b}

![Photograph](figures/imaging/photoSphere.jpg){width="90%" #fig-rendering-c}

Fig (a and b) Two different renderings of a sphere.  (c) Note the many extra details required of a photorealistic rendering.

:::

In general, surface reflection behaves linearly: the reflection from the
sum of two light sources is the sum of the reflections from the
individual sources.

To associate the reflected light with surfaces in the world, we need to
know which light rays came from which direction in space. That requires
that we form an image, which we discuss next.

elements: central 3D sphere with custom shader material supporting Lambertian, Phong specular, and combined Phong shading
two directional light sources positioned to match the two highlights in the image
interactive vector overlay displayed on hover: normal vector (n), view vector (v), reflection vector (r), and light vectors (l1, l2)
subtle dashed lines connecting the hovered surface point to the light positions and the camera

interactions.label: Component
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates how the Phong reflection model decomposes into a view-independent diffuse (Lambertian) component and a view-dependent specular highlight.

interactions.label: Shininess (α)
interactions.teaches: Controls the Phong exponent α. High values create a tiny, sharp highlight (highly polished surface), while low values spread the specular reflection across a wider area (rougher, matte surface).
Allows orbiting the scene to observe how the specular highlights shift position dynamically relative to your viewpoint, while the Lambertian shading remains locked to the light source directions.

demo_steps.title: Two-Source Phong Reflection
demo_steps.narration: This 3D sphere is illuminated by two directional light sources. The two shiny patches are specular highlights, where the light's reflection vector points directly toward your eye.

demo_steps.title: The Lambertian Component
demo_steps.narration: Switching to Lambertian shading isolates the diffuse reflection. Notice how the highlights completely disappear. Diffuse reflection scatters light equally in all directions, depending only on the angle between the surface normal and the light sources.

demo_steps.title: Isolating Phong Specular
demo_steps.narration: This is the pure Phong specular component. It isolates the term (r · q)^α, showing only the glossy highlights. Hover over the sphere to see the vector math: reflection vector (r) and view direction (q) aligning at the center of each highlight.

demo_steps.title: Increasing Surface Shininess
demo_steps.narration: By raising the exponent α to 100, the specular reflection becomes highly directional. The highlights shrink and sharpen, representing a smooth, polished material like glossy plastic or glazed ceramic.

demo_steps.title: Spreading the Highlight
demo_steps.narration: Reducing α to 10 widens the specular lobe. The highlight diffuses over a larger region, simulating a rougher surface where microscopic imperfections scatter the reflected rays in a wider cone.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Straight-on orthographic view of the sphere centered in the frame, matching the flat background context of the original figure.

camera_view.view_notes: The camera looks straight along the z-axis. This perfectly reproduces the silhouette of the textbook sphere. The user can then orbit to see the 3D depth and highlight shifts.

inline_constraints.default_view: Must show a single grey sphere centered in a dark grey background, matching the silhouette, scale, and lighting of 'spherePhongRoughness0.3.png'.
inline_constraints.visible_ui: Position the 'Component' toggle and 'Shininess (α)' slider at the bottom edge to keep the canvas clean.
inline_constraints.explainers: Hovering over the sphere draws the interactive normal (n), light (l), view (v), and reflection (r) vectors at the cursor point to visually explain the Phong math.
inline_constraints.framing: Square aspect ratio, dark gray background color matching the original image environment (#444444).
inline_constraints.camera: Lock to orthographic face-on initially; allow user to orbit to break the flat appearance and discover the 3D geometry.

interactionPlan.notes: Use a custom Three.js ShaderMaterial or MeshPhongMaterial to accurately compute the two light contributions. Set the first light at coordinates (-2.0, 2.0, 3.0) and the second light at (2.5, 0.5, 3.0) to replicate the exact highlight locations shown in the textbook figure. The hover vector overlay must project 3D arrows onto the screen space at the raycast intersection point.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The background is white instead of dark gray.
The specular highlights do not match the original; the reference has two distinct, balanced circular highlights while the generated output has a distorted rim-like highlight and a single sharp dot.
The base sphere shading has too high contrast with a harsh dark shadow on the right side, whereas the original is softly lit.
evaluation.notes: The sphere geometry and interactive shader logic are robust, but the background color and lighting setup are unfaithful to the original's visual style.
action_items: Set the scene background color to a dark gray matching the source image.
Adjust the position of the two light sources so the specular highlights are horizontally balanced and correctly positioned.
Tweak the shader's diffuse and ambient terms to replicate the softer transition and darker material look of the reference sphere.
Refine the default shininess value so the specular highlights match the soft, wide blur seen in the reference.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and geometry are accurate, but the lighting setup, background color, and shader parameters need precise adjustments to match the visual style of the reference.
action_items: Set the scene background color to a dark gray matching the source image.
Adjust the position of the two light sources so the specular highlights are horizontally balanced and correctly positioned.
Tweak the shader's diffuse and ambient terms to replicate the softer transition and darker material look of the reference sphere.
Refine the default shininess value so the specular highlights match the soft, wide blur seen in the reference.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The specular highlight on the right is much dimmer and larger than the one in the original figure.
The sphere is shifted slightly to the right of the center.
The background color is slightly lighter than the dark charcoal of the original.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive features, but the specular highlights do not match the intensity and balance of the source image.
action_items: Adjust the position and intensity of the second light source to make the right highlight bright and sharp.
Center the sphere perfectly within the viewport.
Darken the background color to match the original dark gray tone.

feedback.rationale: The overall representation is highly accurate, but the core visual topic (specular highlights) requires precise tuning of light positions, sphere centering, and background contrast, which can be easily resolved by refining the generator's parameters.

Darken the background color to match the original dark gray tone.

attempts.html:
Hover over sphere for vectors. Orbit to view.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The second specular highlight on the right side of the sphere is completely missing.
The right half of the sphere is in deep shadow, whereas in the original figure it is illuminated and contains a highlight.
The left highlight is shifted too close to the center compared to the original, which is more towards the upper-left edge.
An intrusive UI panel is visible in the bottom-left corner.
evaluation.notes: The sphere geometry and interactive features are excellent, but the lighting fails to recreate the iconic two-highlight look of the original textbook figure, leaving the right side in complete shadow.
action_items: Adjust the position of the second light source (light 2) further to the right and front to bring out the missing highlight on the right.
Increase the ambient light term or add a subtle fill light so the right hemisphere of the sphere is not completely black.
Adjust the position of light 1 to move its specular highlight closer to the upper-left edge as seen in the source image.
Ensure both highlights are clearly visible and match the proportions and positions of the original figure upon initial load.

feedback.rationale: The core geometry and interactive features are excellent, but the lighting execution fails to replicate the double highlight of the original; this can be resolved by refining the light positions and ambient settings in the generator.
action_items: Adjust the position of the second light source (light 2) further to the right and front to bring out the missing highlight on the right.
Increase the ambient light term or add a subtle fill light so the right hemisphere of the sphere is not completely black.
Adjust the position of light 1 to move its specular highlight closer to the upper-left edge as seen in the source image.
Ensure both highlights are clearly visible and match the proportions and positions of the original figure upon initial load.
